content:
First, observe that since each \(a_i\in(2.5,3)\), any reasonable integer approximation must be either 2 or 3.  If we let
\[
d_i = a_i - 2,\quad s_i = 3 - a_i,
\]
then choosing \(A_i=2\) gives error \(d_i\), and choosing \(A_i=3\) gives error \(s_i\).  We have to pick exactly two of the \(A_i\) to be 2 and five to be 3 (so that the total is \(2\cdot2+5\cdot3=19\)), and we want to make
\[
M=\max_i|A_i-a_i|
\]
as small as possible.

Compute the \(d_i\) and \(s_i\).  
\[
\begin{array}{c|ccccccc}
i & 1 &2 &3 &4 &5 &6 &7\\\hline
a_i &2.56&2.61&2.65&2.71&2.79&2.82&2.86\\
d_i=a_i-2&0.56&0.61&0.65&0.71&0.79&0.82&0.86\\
s_i=3-a_i&0.44&0.39&0.35&0.29&0.21&0.18&0.14
\end{array}
\]

To keep \(\max_i|A_i-a_i|\le M\), any index assigned to 2 must have \(d_i\le M\), and any index assigned to 3 must have \(s_i\le M\).  We need two indices with \(d_i\le M\) and five indices with \(s_i\le M\).  

–  There are exactly two \(d_i\)’s \(\le0.61\), namely \(d_1=0.56\) and \(d_2=0.61\).  
–  For those five we assign to 3, all their \(s_i\)’s are \(\le0.39\le0.61\).  

Thus the best we can do is pick \(A_1=A_2=2\), \(A_3=\cdots=A_7=3\).  Then
\[
\max_i|A_i-a_i|=\max\{d_2,s_3\}=\max\{0.61,\,0.35\}=0.61,
\]
and hence \(M=0.61\).  Finally
\[
100M=100\times0.61=61.
\]

\boxed{61}

message.content:
First, observe that since each \(a_i\in(2.5,3)\), any reasonable integer approximation must be either 2 or 3.  If we let
\[
d_i = a_i - 2,\quad s_i = 3 - a_i,
\]
then choosing \(A_i=2\) gives error \(d_i\), and choosing \(A_i=3\) gives error \(s_i\).  We have to pick exactly two of the \(A_i\) to be 2 and five to be 3 (so that the total is \(2\cdot2+5\cdot3=19\)), and we want to make
\[
M=\max_i|A_i-a_i|
\]
as small as possible.

Compute the \(d_i\) and \(s_i\).  
\[
\begin{array}{c|ccccccc}
i & 1 &2 &3 &4 &5 &6 &7\\\hline
a_i &2.56&2.61&2.65&2.71&2.79&2.82&2.86\\
d_i=a_i-2&0.56&0.61&0.65&0.71&0.79&0.82&0.86\\
s_i=3-a_i&0.44&0.39&0.35&0.29&0.21&0.18&0.14
\end{array}
\]

To keep \(\max_i|A_i-a_i|\le M\), any index assigned to 2 must have \(d_i\le M\), and any index assigned to 3 must have \(s_i\le M\).  We need two indices with \(d_i\le M\) and five indices with \(s_i\le M\).  

–  There are exactly two \(d_i\)’s \(\le0.61\), namely \(d_1=0.56\) and \(d_2=0.61\).  
–  For those five we assign to 3, all their \(s_i\)’s are \(\le0.39\le0.61\).  

Thus the best we can do is pick \(A_1=A_2=2\), \(A_3=\cdots=A_7=3\).  Then
\[
\max_i|A_i-a_i|=\max\{d_2,s_3\}=\max\{0.61,\,0.35\}=0.61,
\]
and hence \(M=0.61\).  Finally
\[
100M=100\times0.61=61.
\]

\boxed{61}
